trust_assumption: A developer using an AI coding assistant trusts that an inline completion offered in their editor is a neutral synthesis of best practice, so a snippet accepted with a single keystroke inherits the developer's authorship identity, passes through review as 'code I wrote', and is committed as if a human had reasoned about its security properties.
violation: An attacker biases the assistant's suggestions toward a subtly insecure or backdoored pattern (by seeding public repos, docs, or Stack-Overflow-style answers that the model retrieves or was trained on, or by poisoning the project-context the assistant indexes) so the generated completion carries an injected weakness, and because the accept-completion gesture launders machine-authored text into human-attributed commits, the insecure code crosses the review boundary wearing the developer's identity and is never scrutinized as third-party content.

techniques.name: Suggestion-bias seeding of public corpus to steer completions
Accept-keystroke authorship laundering past code review

rationale: By 2025-2027 a large fraction of new code is first drafted by inline assistants and accepted with a tab, but the entire trust apparatus of software engineering - code review, blame, attribution, ownership - assumes a human authored what is attributed to them. The seam is that the accept gesture collapses a provenance boundary: machine-generated, attacker-influenceable text is relabeled as human-written and thereby exempted from the third-party-code scrutiny that a copy-pasted library or vendored dependency would receive. This is distinct from generic prompt injection and from agent-skill typosquat because the violated trust is the authorship/attribution boundary inside the editor, not a tool call or a package name; the attacker need not compromise any dependency, only bias the suggestion distribution. No SAST tool treats AI-suggested lines as a separate provenance class, and no review process flags 'this hunk was machine-generated' as a risk signal.
suggested_research: Instrument an editor to tag the provenance of every accepted completion and feed that into review, then measure whether reviewers scrutinize machine-authored hunks less; red-team a major assistant by seeding a poisoned pattern in its retrieval corpus and measuring the rate at which the insecure variant is suggested and accepted versus a secure baseline.

trust_assumption: A developer or build agent trusts that a package name confidently emitted by an LLM in an import statement or install command refers to a real, vetted artifact, so the name is passed straight to the package manager as an authoritative identity without first checking that the package exists, who owns it, or whether it was named by a human at all.
violation: An attacker harvests the stable set of non-existent package names that popular models repeatedly hallucinate, pre-registers those exact names on the public registry with malicious payloads, and then any subsequent developer or autonomous build agent that acts on the same hallucinated suggestion installs and executes attacker code - the model's fabricated identity becomes a real, resolvable supply-chain identity the attacker controls.

techniques.name: Pre-registration of recurrent LLM-hallucinated package names
Autonomous-agent install of unverified hallucinated dependency

rationale: Hallucinated package names from LLMs are not random - the same models produce the same fabricated names repeatedly, giving an attacker a predictable, pre-computable target set to squat before any victim exists. As coding agents move toward autonomously resolving and installing dependencies in 2025-2027, the human sanity-check that once caught a weird import disappears, and the model's confident-but-false naming becomes an unmediated authority signal to the package manager. This is distinct from classic typosquatting (which targets human fat-finger errors near real names) and from dependency confusion (which exploits internal/external name resolution order): here the violated trust is that an LLM-emitted name corresponds to any real maintainer identity at all, and the attacker manufactures that identity to match the model's fiction. Registries do not flag never-before-seen names that match known-hallucination corpora, and agent tooling does not gate installs on existence/ownership age.
suggested_research: Build a continuously-updated corpus of model-specific hallucinated package names by sampling completions across languages, cross-reference against fresh registrations to detect adversarial pre-squatting, and prototype an install-time gate for agents that refuses packages whose name first appeared after a recent cutoff or lacks maintainer history.

trust_assumption: An ML engineer pulling a model from a public hub trusts that the accompanying config, tokenizer, and serialized weight artifacts are inert data describing a network, so the loading library is allowed to parse and instantiate them with full local privileges as if deserializing a benign document rather than executing untrusted code.
violation: An attacker publishes a model whose artifact embeds executable behavior at load time - a pickle/`__reduce__` payload, a custom-code-on-load config flag, or a tokenizer/processor that runs arbitrary code during instantiation - so the act of merely loading the 'data' executes attacker code on the engineer's host or in the inference container, exploiting the format boundary between 'a model file' and 'a program'.

techniques.name: Pickle/__reduce__ payload in serialized model artifact

rationale: Model hubs distribute hundreds of thousands of community artifacts and the dominant loading libraries will, by default or with a single flag, deserialize pickles and execute custom model/processor code at load time. The seam is the format-boundary assumption that a downloaded model is passive data when it is actually a program the loader runs with local privilege. This is explicitly distinct from the excluded LoRA-adapter-provenance gap (which concerns who fine-tuned an adapter and behavioral backdoors) - here the violation is pure deserialization/code-execution at load, before any inference happens, regardless of model behavior. Safetensors mitigates the weight-blob case but config, tokenizer, processor, and remote-code paths remain executable, and scanners that flag pickles do not cover the custom-code-on-load vectors. There is no consistent attestation that a hub artifact is execution-free.
suggested_research: Survey the top model artifacts for load-time code paths beyond weight pickles (remote-code configs, custom processors, tokenizer hooks), build a sandboxed 'dry-load' harness that traces any code execution during model instantiation, and propose an execution-free artifact attestation that hubs could enforce.

trust_assumption: An enterprise buyer or platform selecting a model trusts that a published benchmark or leaderboard score measures generalization to unseen tasks, so the score is treated as a procurement-grade fitness signal and used to justify deploying the model into security-relevant workflows.
violation: A model vendor contaminates the benchmark by training (or fine-tuning) on the public test set or near-duplicates of it, so the reported score reflects memorization rather than capability, and the buyer procures and trusts a model whose real-world behavior - including its security guardrail efficacy and tool-use reliability - is materially worse than the number that drove the purchase, with the gap invisible until production.

techniques.name: Benchmark test-set contamination inflating procurement score
Near-duplicate leakage to game held-out evaluation

rationale: In 2025-2027 model selection by enterprises and integrators is heavily driven by leaderboard rank, yet public benchmarks leak into training corpora and vendors face strong incentive to contaminate, turning a measurement into a marketing artifact. The trust violated is procurement-specific: a buyer treats a held-out score as evidence of fitness for a security-relevant deployment, but a contaminated score certifies memorization, not the safety/capability the buyer is actually buying. This is distinct from the excluded eval-set-overfit-bypass (which is about an attacker bypassing a deployed model's eval-tuned defenses) and from LLM-judge gaming (which targets the grader): here the seam is the supply-chain provenance of the score itself as a purchasing trust signal, landing on the human/organizational buyer and the cloud platform that onboards the model. No procurement process demands contamination attestation, and contamination detection is research-stage, not a buyer's-side control.
suggested_research: Develop a buyer-side contamination probe using freshly-authored private held-out tasks and canary strings to estimate the gap between a vendor's leaderboard score and clean-set performance, and define a procurement attestation standard requiring vendors to disclose training-data cutoffs and benchmark exclusion proofs.

trust_assumption: A model trainer running RLHF, DPO, or constitutional/preference fine-tuning trusts that crowdsourced or community preference labels are honest human judgments of helpfulness and harmlessness, so the aggregated preference signal is allowed to directly shape the model's reward function and post-trained behavior without per-labeler provenance or adversarial vetting.
violation: A coordinated set of labelers (or a sybil farm, or automated raters) injects a poisoned preference pattern - upvoting responses that comply with a specific trigger phrase, or systematically rewarding an unsafe behavior framed as 'helpful' - so the trigger-conditioned backdoor is baked into the reward model and inherited by every model trained against it, a behavioral compromise introduced through the feedback channel rather than the training corpus.

techniques.name: Sybil preference-label injection into reward model

rationale: Preference data is increasingly crowdsourced, community-contributed (open preference datasets, in-product thumbs-up/down, arena votes), and even synthetically generated, and it feeds the reward model that most directly governs a model's safety behavior. The seam is that the feedback channel is treated as an honest aggregate human signal while it is in fact an open write surface where a coordinated minority can shift the reward landscape and plant a trigger-gated backdoor that survives into downstream fine-tunes. This is distinct from training-data reconstruction and from the contamination/procurement gap because the violated trust is the integrity of the human-preference channel itself, and the payload is a conditional behavioral backdoor in the reward model rather than data leakage or an inflated score. There is no per-labeler provenance, sybil-resistance, or adversarial audit standard for preference datasets, and no detector for trigger-conditioned reward anomalies.
suggested_research: Construct a poisoned preference subset with a rare trigger phrase, train a reward model and a DPO policy on it, and measure backdoor persistence and stealth; then prototype a preference-data auditor that clusters labeler behavior to surface coordinated voting and trigger-correlated reward shifts.

trust_assumption: A model-as-a-service provider trusts that paying, authenticated API traffic represents legitimate inference consumption, so it serves high-volume, diverse, logit-or-text-rich responses to any account in good standing without treating the query distribution itself as a potential exfiltration of the model's learned function.
violation: An attacker (or a competitor) operates one or many authenticated accounts to systematically query the model across a coverage-maximizing input distribution and trains a substitute model on the responses, extracting a usable clone of the provider's proprietary capability - the provider's identity/billing trust signal authorized the very access that distills its core asset out through the front door.

techniques.name: Coverage-maximizing query farming for model distillation
Sybil-account fan-out to evade per-key extraction limits

rationale: Frontier capability is expensive to train but cheap to distill: a smaller student model trained on a teacher's API outputs can recover much of its behavior, and in 2025-2027 this is both a competitive-IP threat and a way to clone a model along with its safety alignment for offline jailbreak research. The seam is that provider trust is keyed on authentication and billing - a paid, well-behaved account - while the threat lives in the aggregate query distribution, which no per-request authorization sees. This is distinct from training-data reconstruction (which recovers training examples) because the target here is the model function itself, extracted through legitimate inference, and distinct from generic rate-limiting because sybil fan-out and patient pacing defeat per-key thresholds. Providers lack distribution-level extraction detection, and there is no standard for watermarking outputs to prove a downstream clone was distilled.
suggested_research: Simulate distillation against a hosted model under realistic rate limits and sybil fan-out, quantify queries-to-clone-fidelity, and prototype a fleet-level detector that scores account or account-cluster query distributions for coverage-maximizing extraction patterns plus an output-watermarking scheme that survives distillation.

trust_assumption: A team building an agent trusts a shared system-prompt or agent-definition pulled from a community repo, gist, or prompt-marketplace as a configuration template, so it is dropped into the agent's privileged instruction context without the code-review, signing, or dependency-pinning that an executable dependency would receive.
violation: An attacker publishes or updates a popular shared prompt / agent-definition that embeds hidden directives - an instruction to exfiltrate context, to silently prefer an attacker-controlled tool or MCP server, or to lower its own guardrails on a trigger - and because the importing team treats prose configuration as inert text rather than as control-plane code, the agent inherits the attacker's instructions at the highest privilege level it has.

techniques.name: Hidden directive embedded in shared agent-definition

rationale: System prompts and agent definitions are the highest-authority instruction layer an agent has, and a thriving ecosystem of shared prompt repos, awesome-lists, and prompt marketplaces means teams routinely copy them in unreviewed. The seam is that this prose is treated as documentation/config rather than as the control-plane code it actually is, so it bypasses every supply-chain control (signing, pinning, SCA, review) that an imported library triggers. This is distinct from the excluded agent-skill-typosquat (a malicious named skill/tool package) and MCP-capability-drift (post-install permission expansion): here the artifact is a natural-language instruction blob whose danger is invisible to code scanners, and its update channel is unmonitored. No SCA tool parses system prompts as a dependency, no signing standard covers agent definitions, and there is no detection for a prompt update that adds an exfiltration or tool-steering directive.
suggested_research: Build a prompt-dependency scanner that diffs imported system prompts/agent definitions for newly-added directives matching exfiltration, tool-steering, or guardrail-relaxation intent, and propose a signing-and-pinning convention so agent definitions carry the same provenance guarantees as packages.

trust_assumption: An ops team using an agent to generate Terraform, Kubernetes manifests, or cloud IaC trusts that infrastructure-as-code authored by the assistant embodies the same policy intent and least-privilege defaults a human engineer would apply, so the generated plan is approved on a quick skim and applied with the team's deployment authority.
violation: The agent generates infrastructure that is syntactically valid and superficially matches the request but quietly diverges from policy - an over-broad IAM principal, a public-by-default storage resource, a disabled control plane log, a wildcard security-group - and because reviewers trust agent-authored IaC as policy-aligned and policy-as-code gates do not cover the specific drift, the non-compliant infrastructure is applied with full authority and becomes live attack surface.

techniques.name: Policy-as-code gap exploited by plausible agent IaC drift

rationale: Agents authoring and even applying IaC is a fast-growing 2025-2027 pattern, and the failure mode is not malicious code but plausible-looking infra that drifts from intended policy in ways a skim approval misses. The seam is the provenance trust placed on agent-authored infrastructure: reviewers assume the generator internalized least-privilege and compliance intent, while the agent optimizes for 'works/applies' and policy-as-code gates only catch the rules someone pre-wrote, leaving the long tail of drift unguarded. This is distinct from generic IaC misconfiguration because the new element is the implicit trust that an AI author is policy-aware, which suppresses human scrutiny precisely as volume of generated infra explodes. Detection that diffs agent-generated plans against intended policy posture (not just a fixed ruleset) is nascent, and no provenance signal marks a plan as machine-authored to trigger heavier review.
suggested_research: Generate IaC from agents across many prompts and measure the rate and class of policy drift versus human baselines, then build a review gate that tags plan provenance as agent-authored and runs an intent-vs-policy differential (beyond static OPA rules) before apply.

trust_assumption: An application that fronts its LLM with a safety/guardrail classifier (prompt-injection detector, content moderator, jailbreak filter) trusts that this safety model is a faithful enforcing dependency, so it relies on the classifier's verdict to authorize or block inputs and outputs without independently verifying the classifier artifact's integrity or that its decisions still hold.
violation: An attacker swaps, poisoned-fine-tunes, or version-pins-backward the guardrail classifier in the supply chain - publishing a malicious build under the same model id, compromising the hub artifact, or seeding a backdoor that passes a specific trigger - so the safety layer silently approves the very inputs/outputs it exists to block, and the application keeps trusting its verdicts while the protection has been hollowed out.

techniques.name: Backdoored guardrail model passing trigger-marked attacks

rationale: Guardrail and moderation models are increasingly shipped as off-the-shelf dependencies (open-weight safety classifiers, hub-hosted moderation models, vendor SDKs) and treated as a trusted enforcement boundary, but they are themselves an ML supply-chain artifact subject to swap, poisoning, and silent regression. The seam is that the security control's own integrity and decision-fidelity are unverified: the application trusts the classifier's allow/block output as authoritative while a compromised or backdoored build can be made to wave through attacker-marked content. This is distinct from generic model-hub deserialization (which is load-time code execution) and from LLM-judge gaming (which games a benign grader's metric): here the target is the safety enforcer as a dependency whose compromise produces a fail-open that no upstream control sees. There is no continuous attestation that the deployed guardrail behaves as certified and no detection for a guardrail that has silently started passing a trigger class.
suggested_research: Train a backdoored guardrail classifier that fails open on a hidden trigger and measure detectability, then build a continuous guardrail-canary harness that feeds known-bad red-team inputs to the live safety model and alarms on any drop in block rate, plus an artifact-attestation pin so the deployed classifier matches its certified build.
